title: Sticky toffee traybake
subtitle: The pub pudding in slab form
excerpt: Sticky toffee pudding's flavours in a traybake format: date-enriched sponge with toffee sauce that serves a crowd and cuts into squares. Easier to unmould than the individual pudding version and just as good warm with cream.
sourceNotes: Sticky toffee pudding is attributed to various British sources from the mid-twentieth century, with the Sharrow Bay Hotel in the Lake District among the most cited for popularising it. The traybake format follows the same date-sponge and toffee-sauce structure but is baked in a rectangular tin rather than in individual basins.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked sponge without sauce for up to 2 months. Make fresh toffee sauce to serve. Reheat from frozen at 160°C for 15 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge can be baked a day ahead. Reheat squares in the oven at 160°C for 10 minutes and pour hot toffee sauce over just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried dates soaked in boiling water with bicarbonate of soda then blended to a smooth paste. The soaking liquid keeps; both go into the sponge batter. The bicarbonate reacts with the date's natural acids to produce carbon dioxide, which lightens the batter.

content.text: The sponge base is moist and deeply flavoured by the

stirred through it — a soft, giving crumb that absorbs toffee sauce readily. Pour some of the sauce over the hot sponge to saturate the top before serving, and offer the rest warm in a jug. The traybake format means you skip the individual-pudding unmoulding anxiety; the sponge stays in its tin and squares are cut to order.

Pour the boiling water over the chopped dates and bicarbonate of soda. Leave for 10 minutes, then blend or mash to a rough purée. The bicarbonate colours the water dark and reacts with the dates' acids; this is correct.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Grease and line a 30 × 20 cm baking tin. Beat the softened butter and muscovado sugar together until combined — muscovado is damp and doesn't cream as smoothly as caster sugar; 3 minutes is enough. Add the eggs one at a time, then the vanilla. Fold in the flour and baking powder, then stir in the date purée. The batter will be fairly liquid. Pour into the prepared tin.

Bake for 28–35 minutes until the top is set, feels springy when pressed gently, and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. The sponge should be a deep brown, pulling slightly away from the tin edges.

While the sponge bakes, melt the butter and muscovado together in a saucepan over a medium heat. Once dissolved, add the cream and salt and bring to a gentle bubble. Simmer for 3 minutes until slightly thickened. Keep warm.

Pour half the warm toffee sauce over the hot sponge as soon as it comes from the oven. Leave to soak for 5 minutes, then cut into squares and serve with the remaining sauce. Offer cream or vanilla ice cream alongside.
